title: Pathways to Policing Act
plain_summary: The Pathways to Policing Act creates a program to help police departments find and hire more officers. The federal government will give money to states and local police departments to pay for ads and special training programs that attract new people to become police officers. The bill especially wants to recruit people from communities that don't have many police officers. The Attorney General will also run big advertising campaigns across the whole country to get more people interested in police jobs.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Pathways to Policing Act.

sections.title: Section 2 Grant Program
sections.text: This section adds a new part to an existing crime control law from nineteen sixty eight. The new part is called Law Enforcement Hiring Support Grant. The COPS Director (the head of the federal Community Oriented Policing Services office) can give out grants to help hire more police officers. Within one hundred eighty days after this law passes, the COPS Director can start giving money to states, local governments, and police departments through a competition process. To get money, these groups must fill out an application at the time and in the way the COPS Director requires. The money can be used for two main things. First, up to half the grant money can pay for advertising and recruiting campaigns to get people interested in becoming police officers. Second, up to half the grant money can pay for Pathways to Policing programs. These are special programs that help people become police officers even if they face barriers like lack of education or training. The programs can provide money for school tuition, pay, or benefits while people learn to become officers. The programs can also include part time uniformed jobs that help train future full time officers. Every year that someone gets grant money, they must send the COPS Director a report about how they used the money. When giving out grants, the COPS Director must give priority to groups that want to recruit people from communities that do not have many police officers or people with unusual educational or work backgrounds. The COPS Director must also give priority to groups that want to recruit people who live in or will move to the communities they will serve as police officers. Fifty million dollars is approved to be spent on this grant program each year from twenty twenty six to twenty thirty. The law also requires the Attorney General to create and run nationwide advertising and recruiting campaigns within one year of this law passing. These campaigns should encourage people to become police officers, especially people from underrepresented communities or with nontraditional backgrounds. The Attorney General must work with state attorneys general, police groups, professional police associations, community organizations, and researchers when creating these campaigns. The Attorney General must also create educational materials and resources that states, local governments, and police departments can use for their own recruiting campaigns. Another fifty million dollars is approved to be spent each year from twenty twenty six to twenty thirty for these nationwide campaigns.
